info.headline: Flood Watch for Warburton River
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

info.headline: Flood Watch for Cooper Creek
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

info.headline: Flood Watch for Lake Eyre
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. info.headline: Flood Watch for Finke River and Stephenson Creek
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

info.headline: Flood Watch for Simpson Desert
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.

info.headline: Flood Watch for Warburton District Rivers
info.description:
FURTHER FLOODING POSSIBLE IN PARTS OF THE NORTH AND NORTH-WEST CATCHMENTS FROM TUESDAY
A tropical low situated near the Northern Territory border is feeding tropical moisture along a surface trough to its southeast. Meanwhile an upper trough is moving across the southwest of South Australia. The combination of these two systems is continuing to bring the risk of heavy rainfall to northeastern parts of the state during Tuesday and Wednesday.
Catchments in the Flood Watch area are moderately wet and are likely to respond quickly to rainfall.
Forecast rainfall may cause further river and creek level rises, prolonged overland flooding, and ponding from Tuesday onwards. Roads access may be affected, and some communities and homesteads my become isolated. Check road conditions before travelling.
info.instruction: * Don't drive, walk, swim or play in floodwater because it is dangerous. * Stay away from flooded drains, rivers, streams and waterways. * Obey road closure signs. Plan ahead so you don't drive on flooded roads. * Check the ABC and local media for updates. The situation can change quickly, so stay informed. * For local emergency management warnings and advice visit www.ses.sa.gov.au/incidents-and-warnings/incidents-and-warnings-map/.
